version_relationship.relationship: Same counting problem and structural characterization. F1 has t <= 10000 and sum(m) <= 100000, so an upper-bound-dependent descending divisor DP may be run separately per query; F2 raises m to 1000000, t to 300000, and removes the sum(m) bound, forcing one reusable ascending precomputation.

sources.notes: Read the complete statement, definition of f(k), example, input/output, and easy constraints: t <= 10000 and 1 <= m with sum(m) <= 100000.

Read the complete official F1 tutorial and code. It proves the divisibility chain, logarithmic length bound, 2^(n-1) deque arrangements, strict suffix-GCD reduction, and descending divisor-inversion DP; it also gives the length-free weighted optimization.
Actually read the full independent F1 article. It derives the single-valley structure, exact-gcd transition, Mobius inversion over divisors, maintained multiple sums, and O(sum tau(i)^2) complexity.

Actually read the full cross-version article. Its Cartesian-tree argument independently shows that every subtree size must occur, hence the max Cartesian tree is a chain and each chosen value set has 2^(n-1) valid arrangements; it also explains why the F1 descent cannot be shared across F2 queries.

Independently proved the adjacent-maximum support argument and weighted GCD-DP recurrence. An in-memory exhaustive check enumerated all arrays for m <= 9 and lengths through 5, matching the strict-suffix-GCD characterization and counts 4, 29, and 165 for the samples; no files or database rows were written.

annotation.summary: For every nonempty length, count arrays over [1,m] for which the GCDs of all length-k subarray maxima are pairwise distinct. This is the same combinatorial object as F2, but F1's aggregate bound permits a separate descending DP for each query.
annotation.constraints: 1 <= t <= 10000; 1 <= m and the sum of m over all tests is at most 100000; answers are modulo 998244353. The time limit is 4 seconds and the memory limit is 500 MB.
annotation.core_idea: Let s_k be the sequence of length-k window maxima and g_k=gcd(s_k). Since s_{k+1} is obtained by replacing adjacent pairs of s_k by their maximum, every entry of s_{k+1} is an entry of s_k and g_k divides g_{k+1}. Distinct g_k therefore form a strict divisibility chain, so n <= floor(log2(m))+1. Moreover, the supports of s_1,s_2,... form n nested sets with a strict loss at every step; because s_1 has at most n distinct values and s_n has one, the original values are all distinct and every step deletes exactly the current minimum. Thus good arrays are exactly single-valley arrays obtained by choosing b_1<...<b_n and inserting each b_i (i>=2) at either deque end, giving 2^(n-1) arrangements. Their g_k are gcd(b_k,...,b_n), so all suffix GCDs must be distinct. Process a possible new minimum i from m down to 1, aggregate already-built increasing chains by their GCD h, and prepend i only when gcd(i,h)<h; this doubles the 2^(length-1) weight. Divisor zeta sums plus Mobius-style subtraction recover each exact gcd(i,h) class without scanning all h.
annotation.complexity: After global divisor-list precomputation, O(sum_{i=1}^m tau(i)^2) time per test and O(m + sum_{i=1}^m tau(i)) memory; over the input, O(sum_tests sum_{i=1}^{m_t} tau(i)^2) time. This is commonly bounded as near O(sum(m) log^3(max m)) for these constraints.
tricks: View longer-window maxima as repeated adjacent-maximum transforms.
Use strict nested support loss to prove distinct values and the single-valley/deque characterization.
Fold the 2^(length-1) arrangement count into DP values, multiplying by 2 on every extension.
Maintain sums over GCD states divisible by each divisor, then invert on the divisor lattice to recover exact gcd classes.
Exclude transitions with h=gcd(i,h), because they repeat a suffix GCD instead of making the chain strict.
uncertainty_points: No unresolved correctness uncertainty remains for the primary F1 variant after the complete statement/editorial reads, two independent Luogu derivations, and exhaustive small-instance checks.

The exact complexity recorded here is the divisor-sum bound O(sum tau(i)^2); informal O(m log^3 m) statements in independent writeups are average-order simplifications, not a per-integer worst-case identity.

The current taxonomy contains both math/number-theory/mobius and math/number-theory/mobius-inversion. This payload uses the latter because its definition specifically covers recovering exact divisibility/GCD classes from cumulative divisor counts and it is the more-used current tag.
No new F1 tag is proposed; all emitted F1 tags already exist in the current taxonomy.
proof_risks: Pairwise distinct g_k is stronger than merely having distinct array values: the selected increasing values must also have strictly changing suffix GCDs.
Calling the array single-valley without proving the nested-support loss is insufficient; the proof must rule out duplicate values and multiple values disappearing in one adjacent-max step.
The factor 2 is already folded into each extension. Multiplying the final DP result by 2^(length-1) again double-counts every arrangement.
Exact-gcd inversion must remove the h=g class. In the optimized recurrence this is the subtraction of the aggregate whose current GCD already equals the target divisor.
The descending state depends on the query's upper bound m. Reusing it naively for a larger m omits chains containing newly introduced larger values.

solution_variants.name: descending-weighted-exact-gcd-dp
solution_variants.summary: Process i from m down to 1. Let A[h] be the total 2^(length-1)-weighted count of valid increasing chains whose first value is greater than i and whose total GCD is h, and maintain Z[d]=sum_{d|h} A[h]. Divisor-lattice inversion extracts E[d]=sum_{gcd(i,h)=d} A[h] for every d|i. Create weight 2(E[d]-A[d]) at GCD d to exclude unchanged h=d, add the singleton chain at d=i, then merge the new states into A and all relevant Z divisors.
solution_variants.complexity: O(sum_{i=1}^m tau(i)^2) time and O(m + sum_{i=1}^m tau(i)) memory after divisor precomputation.

tags.evidence: The answer is computed by a weighted counting DP over the current minimum and exact total GCD; every extension doubles the stored arrangement weight.
Transitions know cumulative counts for GCD states divisible by d and must invert these divisor sums to isolate states with gcd(i,h)=d exactly.
The structural criterion is a strict chain of suffix GCDs, and the DP transition changes state h to gcd(i,h) while rejecting equality.
Precomputed divisor lists and maintained sums over multiples make every exact-GCD transition run on the divisibility poset instead of scanning all values.
After the single-valley reduction, each valid increasing value chain contributes exactly 2^(n-1) left/right deque insertion arrangements.
